author: śrīla saccidānanda bhaktibinoda ṭhākura
verses:
“pūrṇa-cidānanda tumi, tomāra citkaṇa āmi,
svabhāvataḥ āmi tuyā dāsa
parama svatantra tumi, tuyā paratantra āmi,
tuyā pada chāḍi’ sarvanāśa

svatantra ha’ye jakhana, māyā⋅prati kainu mana,
sva⋅svabhāva chāḍila āmāya
prapañce māyāra bandhe, paḍinu karmera dhande,
karma-cakre āmāre phelāya

māyā tava icchāmate, bā̐dhe mora e jagate,
adṛṣṭa⋅nirbandha⋅lauha⋅kare
sei ta’ nirbandha more, āne śrīvāsera ghare,
putrarūpe mālinī⋅jaṭhare

se⋅nirbandha punarāya, more ebe la’ye jāya,
āmi ta’ thākite nāri āra
tava icchā suprabala, mora icchā sudurbala,
āmi jīva akiñcana chāra

jathāya pāṭhāo tumi, avaśya jāiba āmi,
kā’ra kebā putra⋅pati⋅pitā
jaḍera sambandha saba, tāhā nāhi satya saba,
tumi jīvera nitya pālayitā

saṁyoga⋅viyoge jini, sukha⋅duḥkha mane gaṇi’,
tava pade chāḍena āśraya
māyāra gardabha ha’ye, majena saṁsāra la’ye”,
bhaktivinodera sei bhaya
en_translation:
You are full of eternal bliss and knowledge, and I am a tiny particle of Your consciousness. Naturally, I am Your servant. You are supremely independent, and I am dependent on You. To leave Your feet is complete ruin.

When I desired to be independent and turned my mind toward illusion, my true nature left me. In this material world, bound by illusion, I fell into the confusion of fruitive action. The cycle of karma throws me about.

By Your will, illusion binds me in this world with the iron shackles of destiny. That very destiny brought me to the house of Śrīvāsa as a son in the womb of Mālinī.

That destiny now takes me away again. I cannot stay any longer. Your will is supremely powerful, and my will is very weak. I am a lowly, insignificant soul.

Wherever You send me, I will certainly go. Who is anyone's son, husband, or father? All these material relationships are not ultimately real. You are the eternal maintainer of the soul.

One who, in union or separation, considers happiness and distress in the mind and forsakes shelter at Your feet becomes a beast of burden for illusion and is absorbed in the material world." That is the fear of Bhaktivinoda.
